skill.name: brainstorming
skill.description: Creative exploration and brainstorming; use it when you need to clarify goals/constraints, explore multiple options, and converge on a direction before implementation.
skill.summary: A structured brainstorming guide that helps you clarify goals, generate options, evaluate trade-offs, and converge on actionable plans.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 17 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a markdown-only brainstorming instruction document with no executable code, no network calls, no file system access, and no environment variable access. The scanner confused markdown backtick formatting with shell execution, and text about brainstorming workflows with cryptographic algorithms and system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm (false positive - text pattern match)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged plain text review notes and workflow descriptions as cryptographic algorithm usage. Lines flagged include YAML frontmatter descriptions and archived audit notes. No cryptographic code exists in the skill.

low_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution (false positive - markdown formatting)
low_findings.description: Static scanner confused markdown backtick formatting and fenced code blocks with Ruby shell backtick execution. Backticks in this file are used exclusively for markdown formatting of file references and code blocks containing example text.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance (false positive - text pattern match)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged brainstorming workflow guidance text such as interaction rhythm suggestions and delivery standards as system reconnaissance. All flagged text is legitimate brainstorming methodology content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (false positive - source attribution)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged the source repository URL in YAML frontmatter metadata. This URL is transparent project attribution and is not used for data exfiltration or network communication.

content.user_title: Brainstorm ideas with structured creative exploration
content.value_statement: Making decisions with vague ideas is difficult and often leads to incomplete planning. This skill provides a structured process to clarify goals, generate options, evaluate trade-offs, and converge on an actionable direction.
actual_capabilities: Clarify vague goals, identify constraints, and define success criteria
Generate multiple distinct solution options with structured evaluation
Decompose complex problems into components, subproblems, and decision points
Compare options by effort, risk, feasibility, and expected impact
Converge on a prioritized recommendation with concrete next steps
Challenge assumptions and explore alternative approaches to problems
limitations: Does not execute code, build prototypes, or run experiments
Does not access external data sources or real-time information
Quality depends on clear user input about goals and constraints
Not suitable for well-defined tasks that can be executed directly

use_cases.title: Feature brainstorming for product teams
use_cases.description: Product managers can explore multiple feature directions, compare trade-offs, and select the best option within constraints.

use_cases.title: Technical solution design
use_cases.description: Engineers can explore architecture options, evaluate technology trade-offs, and document decision rationale before implementation.

use_cases.title: Strategic planning and goal setting
use_cases.description: Team leads can clarify team objectives, generate strategy options, and define actionable next steps with the team.

prompt_templates.title: Basic brainstorming request
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a vague idea about [describe your idea]. Help me clarify my goals and explore possible directions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a new idea

prompt_templates.title: Brainstorming with constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to [achieve goal]. Constraints: [list constraints]. Goal: [specific metric]. Please brainstorm options, compare them, and recommend a direction with next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-dimensional problem exploration
prompt_templates.prompt: We need to [solve problem]. Consider these dimensions: user needs, implementation path, risk points, and success metrics. For each dimension propose 2-3 options with pros and cons. Then converge on a recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Deep dive with assumption challenging
prompt_templates.prompt: Our team assumes [current assumption]. Challenge this assumption and explore alternatives. For each alternative describe what must be true for it to succeed. Recommend the best direction with validation steps.

output_examples.input: I want to improve user onboarding for our mobile app but I am not sure what approach to take. Constraints: mobile only, no budget for new tools, 3-week timeline.
output_examples.output:
Goal: Improve onboarding completion rate.
Constraints: Mobile only, no new tools, 3-week timeline.

Options:
1. Progressive onboarding tutorial (Medium effort, high clarity)
2. Interactive checklist with rewards (Low effort, engaging)
3. Simplified one-screen signup (Low effort, fastest to ship)

Recommendation: Interactive checklist. It requires no new tools, fits within 3 weeks, and improves retention through gamification.

output_examples.input: Our team is deciding between React Native and Flutter for our next mobile project. Key concerns: development speed, team skills, and long-term maintenance.
output_examples.output:
Goal: Choose a mobile framework.

Dimensions:
- Developer experience: React Native has wider talent pool
- Performance: Flutter offers better rendering
- Ecosystem: Both have mature libraries

Trade-off: React Native reduces hiring risk. Flutter offers better cross-platform consistency.

Recommendation: React Native if team has web background. Flutter if team is new to both.

best_practices: Start by clearly defining the goal, constraints, and success criteria before generating ideas
Diverge first by generating many options, then converge by evaluating trade-offs
Document assumptions and open questions that need validation before committing to a direction
anti_patterns: Jumping to a solution immediately without exploring alternative approaches
Overloading the session with too many constraints or dimensions at once
Treating the recommendation as final without identifying validation steps

faq.question: What kind of problems is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It works best for ill-defined problems, creative ideation, feature planning, and strategic decision making where you need structure to explore options.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code or access the internet?
faq.answer: No. This skill only provides structured instructions for brainstorming. It does not execute code or access external resources.

faq.question: How many options should I expect from a brainstorming session?
faq.answer: The skill typically generates 2 to 4 distinct options with trade-off comparisons, then converges on a single recommendation.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for technical architecture decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. It works well for comparing technology choices, design patterns, and implementation approaches with structured evaluation.

faq.question: How do I get the best results from this skill?
faq.answer: Provide clear goals and constraints in your initial request. The more specific your boundaries, the more relevant the generated options will be.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for team brainstorming sessions?
faq.answer: Yes. The structured output helps teams align on objectives, compare options objectively, and document decision rationale.
